edges.description: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency causes fat malabsorption, manifesting as steatorrhea.
Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and malnutrition contribute to weight loss.
intermediate_mechanisms: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency causes maldigestion and malnutrition.

edges.description: Chronic pancreatitis-associated endocrine pancreatic damage manifests as pancreatogenic diabetes mellitus.

evidence.reference_title: Type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes mellitus secondary to chronic pancreatitis and pancreatic cancer.
evidence.snippet: Diabetes can also develop as a direct consequence of other diseases, including diseases of the exocrine pancreas.

nodes.description: Progressive loss of islet cells leads to diabetes mellitus (type 3c). Often occurs later in disease course.

evidence.reference_title: Type 3c (pancreatogenic) diabetes mellitus secondary to chronic pancreatitis and pancreatic cancer.
evidence.snippet: The most commonly identified causes of type 3c diabetes are chronic pancreatitis, pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, haemochromatosis, cystic fibrosis, and previous pancreatic surgery.

nodes.description: Loss of acinar cell mass leads to insufficient digestive enzyme production. Steatorrhea and malabsorption occur when >90% of exocrine function is lost.

evidence.reference_title: Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency and Malnutrition in Chronic Pancreatitis: Identification, Treatment, and Consequences.
evidence.snippet: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency was diagnosed in 84.6% (77/91) of patients based on symptoms of bloating, steatorrhea, or weight loss.

evidence.reference_title: Exocrine Pancreatic Insufficiency and Malnutrition in Chronic Pancreatitis: Identification, Treatment, and Consequences.
evidence.snippet: There is a high prevalence of fat-soluble vitamin deficiencies, osteopathy, and malnutrition in CP patients, which is underestimated due to a lack of effective diagnosis and suboptimal therapies for EPI.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy in chronic pancreatitis: systematic review and meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: PERT improved coefficient of nitrogen absorption, reduced faecal fat excretion, faecal nitrogen excretion, faecal weight and abdominal pain, without significant adverse events.

evidence.reference_title: Cause and effect relationship of malnutrition with idiopathic chronic pancreatitis: prospective case-control study.
evidence.snippet: After the onset of chronic pancreatitis, 56.5% of patients lost weight and significantly more patients became malnourished compared with controls (45.8% vs 22.5%; P < 0.001).
